36: A direct revelation: 'God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified.' This is the climax of Peter's Pentecost sermon and one of the densest Christological sentences in the NT. Kurios (Lord) carries the full weight of the LXX divine name (YHWH); Christos (Christ/Messiah) the royal-covenantal designation. The resurrection-exaltation is the moment of enthronement — not that Jesus became Lord only then (his pre-existence is assumed throughout Acts' theology), but that the cosmic lordship is now publicly declared and the messianic mission fulfilled. The audience ('whom you crucified') makes the declaration forensically stunning: the crucified one is the enthroned Lord.
1: Pentecost is the fulfillment of Christ's specific promise ('wait for the promise of the Father', Acts 1:4; 'you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit not many days from now', Acts 1:5). The Spirit's arrival is not independent of Christ but is his gift from the Father (Acts 2:33 — 'he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing'). The first day of the new age is the day Christ pours out his Spirit, inaugurating the age of the church. Pentecost is as Christologically driven as Christmas or Easter.
2: The sound of a rushing wind (pnoē biaias) filling the house alludes to the Spirit of God who 'moves over the face of the waters' (Genesis 1:2 — ruach), the breath of life breathed into Adam (Genesis 2:7), and Ezekiel's wind bringing the dry bones to life (Ezekiel 37:9-14). The Christological connection: the risen Christ had breathed on his disciples and said 'Receive the Holy Spirit' (John 20:22) — Pentecost is the full outpouring of what that anticipatory act prefigured.
3: The fire tongues (glōssai hōsei pyros) distribute and rest on each person — not on the building or a symbol but on individuals. John had prophesied that the Coming One would 'baptize with the Holy Spirit and fire' (Luke 3:16). The fire is simultaneously the refining and consuming fire of divine holiness and the illuminating fire of divine presence. Each believer receiving the fire is a Pentecostal anointing — they become individually Christ's temples (1 Corinthians 6:19) just as the cloud of glory once filled Solomon's temple.
4: 'They were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues (heterais glōssais).' The Spirit-filling that drives proclamation is the first Christological act of the new age: the church begins with speech. The content of the speech (Acts 2:11 — 'the mighty works of God') is proclamation, not ecstasy. The Spirit given by the risen Christ immediately empowers witness — the connection between Spirit-reception and bold proclamation is consistent throughout Acts (Acts 2:4; Acts 4:8, 31; Acts 9:17-20; Acts 13:9-12).
5: The Diaspora Jews in Jerusalem for Pentecost represent the full geographic range of Israel's scattering (Acts 2:9-11 list fifteen regions). The Christological significance: the reversal of Diaspora begins at Pentecost. The scattered people are drawn back — not physically to Jerusalem permanently but to the Christ whom Jerusalem's proclamation will announce. The pilgrimage festival becomes the occasion for the gospel's first global-scale proclamation.
6: Each one heard in his own language (tē idią dialektō) — the miracle is not only speaking but hearing comprehensible proclamation. The Babel reversal (Genesis 11:7-9 — language scattered so peoples could not understand one another) is undone: the Spirit of the crucified-and-risen Christ creates intelligibility across linguistic barriers. The kingdom of God speaks every human language simultaneously because Christ's lordship knows no linguistic boundary.
7: 'Amazed and astonished, they said, ‘Are not all these who are speaking Galileans?’' — the messengers are visibly unqualified by cultural prestige. Galilee was considered peripheral to the Jerusalem religious establishment. The Christological irony repeats: God chose what is weak and lowly to shame the strong (1 Corinthians 1:27). The Galilean disciples of a Galilean carpenter, filled with the Spirit of the risen Lord, become the instruments of world-spanning proclamation.
8: The multilingual proclamation creates a unity-in-diversity that is itself a sign of Christ's all-encompassing lordship. The rhetorical question — 'how is it that we each hear in our own native language?' — directs attention to the supernatural character of the event. No Babel-era linguistic unifier; no lingua franca; instead, simultaneous comprehension in every language. The Spirit of Christ translates the gospel without reducing it to one cultural form.
9: Parthians, Medes, Elamites, and residents of Mesopotamia — the eastern Diaspora, beyond the Roman Empire's eastern frontier. The list begins at the far east and works westward, geographically encompassing the entire known Jewish world. The Christological geography: Christ's first Pentecostal harvest includes Jews from every corner of the Roman world and beyond, the first down-payment of the 'ends of the earth' mission (Acts 1:8).
10: Phrygia and Pamphylia (Asia Minor), Egypt and Libya near Cyrene, visitors from Rome — both Jews and proselytes. The Gentile proselytes in the crowd mean that the first recipients of Pentecost's proclamation already include former Gentiles who had converted to Judaism. The Christ-event's effect on the nations begins even before the formal Gentile mission of Acts 10-11. The Spirit overflows ethnic categories from the very first day.
11: 'Cretans and Arabs — we hear them telling in our own tongues the mighty works of God' (tas megaleia tou theou) — the content of the Spirit-filled speech is praise of God's great acts. The phrase echoes the Exodus vocabulary (megaleia in Deuteronomy 11:2 LXX for the mighty acts of the Exodus). The Christological reframe: the mighty works of God the crowd is hearing proclaimed are not the Exodus but the resurrection — the new and greater Exodus accomplished by Christ.
12: 'All were amazed and perplexed, saying to one another, 'What does this mean?'' (ti an theloi touto einai) — the crowd's genuine theological question is exactly the question Peter's sermon answers. The Spirit-event creates the conditions for proclamation; the confusion becomes the opening for explanation. The crowd's perplexity is the Spirit-created invitation for the Christological announcement that follows.
13: The mockers' interpretation — 'they are filled with new wine' — is the persistently reductive reading that reduces divine reality to material explanation. The Christological parallel: Jesus himself was accused of being demon-possessed (Mark 3:22) and mad (John 10:20). The Spirit's coming provokes the same divided response as Jesus's ministry: amazement and faith on one side, dismissal and mockery on the other. Every generation must decide what to do with the Pentecostal evidence.
14: Peter stands up with the Eleven — the reconstituted apostolic community — and raises his voice in public proclamation. The Christological transformation of Peter: the man who could not admit knowing Jesus before a servant girl in a courtyard now stands before thousands in Jerusalem to proclaim the crucified-and-risen Lord. The Spirit-filling has done what three years of discipleship did not: created the boldness that resurrection-witness requires.
15: 'These people are not drunk as you suppose, for it is the third hour of the day' — the defense by time (9 a.m., before the first meal) is the minor point. The major claim is the Christological explanation that replaces the mocking interpretation. Peter's apologetic method is a model: engage the audience's actual charge honestly, then reframe within the scriptural narrative. The Pentecostal phenomenon demands a scriptural explanation, not a sociological one.
16: 'But this is what was uttered through the prophet Joel' — the pesher formula (touto estin to eirēmenon, 'this is that which was spoken') applies Joel 2:28-32 to the Pentecost event. The Christological exegesis: Peter identifies the Spirit-outpouring as the eschatological fulfillment Joel foresaw, and the 'name of the Lord' in Joel 2:32 ('everyone who calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved') as the name of Jesus (Acts 2:21 → Acts 2:38 — 'call on the name of the Lord' becomes 'baptized in the name of Jesus Christ').
17: 'Your sons and daughters will prophesy, your young men will see visions, your old men will dream dreams' — the universality of Spirit-distribution across age and gender is Christ's fulfillment of Moses's wish: 'Would that all the LORD's people were prophets, that the LORD would put his Spirit on them!' (Numbers 11:29). The Christological new covenant: what was exceptional in Israel (the Spirit on prophets, priests, and kings) becomes the birthright of every believer. Christ has made all his people a 'kingdom of priests' (1 Peter 2:9, citing Exodus 19:6).
18: 'Even on my male servants and female servants I will pour out my Spirit, and they shall prophesy' — the social barriers of the old era (gender, status, age) that restricted Spirit-reception are dissolved in the Christological new age. Paul will articulate the theological foundation: 'there is no longer Jew or Greek, slave or free, male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28). Pentecost is the Spirit-instantiation of that Christological equality.
19: 'And I will show wonders in the heavens above and signs on the earth below, blood and fire and vapor of smoke' — the cosmic signs accompanying the Day of the Lord. Peter's citation suggests Pentecost inaugurates the last days; the specific cosmic signs may apply to the Jerusalem destruction of 70 CE (cf. Luke 21:11, 20-24) as the first great historical judgment within the Day of the Lord. The Christological frame: Christ's resurrection has inaugurated the eschatological age; the decisive judgment-event (the cross, then the 70 CE destruction) follows as divine verification.
20: 'The sun shall be turned to darkness and the moon to blood, before the day of the Lord comes, the great and magnificent day' — the Day of the Lord in OT prophecy is consistently tied to divine judgment and salvation (Amos 5:18-20; Joel 1-3; Isa 13; Zeph 1). Peter's application positions the crucifixion (the great darkness, Luke 23:44-45) within this cosmic-judgment vocabulary. The Day of the Lord has arrived in Christ's death and resurrection; all subsequent history moves toward its consummation.
21: 'And it shall come to pass that everyone who calls upon the name of the Lord shall be saved' — Joel's promise of universal salvation through the LORD's name is Peter's bridge to the Christological announcement that follows. In Joel's context, 'the LORD' is YHWH; in Peter's application, 'the Lord' is Jesus (Acts 2:36 — God has made him Lord). The equation is deliberate: to call on the name of Jesus is to call on the name of the LORD. The Christological identification of Jesus with YHWH is embedded in the sermon's exegesis.
22: 'Jesus of Nazareth, a man attested to you by God with mighty works and wonders and signs (dynamesin kai terasi kai sēmeiois) that God did through him in your midst' — Peter establishes the historical factuality of Jesus's ministry as divine attestation. The Christological argument: if God's own power was working through Jesus's miracles (which the crowd could verify — 'as you yourselves know'), then God himself had endorsed Jesus's identity and message before the crucifixion.
23: 'This Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men' — the most theologically precise statement about the crucifixion in Acts. Three elements: (1) divine plan (hōrismenē boulē, the determined counsel of God); (2) human guilt ('you crucified'; 'by the hands of lawless men'); (3) foreknowledge. The cross is simultaneously the most evil act in human history and the most intentional act in divine history. Neither the divine decree cancels human guilt nor human guilt cancels divine sovereignty. The Christological center: Christ's death was neither accident nor defeat but the event God planned from before creation.
24: 'God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death (lysas tas ōdinas tou thanatou), because it was not possible for him to be held by it.' The resurrection is presented as divine inevitability: death could not hold the Author of life (Acts 3:15). The phrase 'pangs of death' (ōdinas) translates a Hebrew phrase sometimes rendered 'cords of Sheol' (Psalms 18:4-5; Acts 116:3) — metaphors of death's constrictive grip. Christ burst free from death's grip not because death was weak but because Christ is life itself. The resurrection is not a reversal of the crucifixion but its necessary completion.
25: The citation of Psalms 16:8-11 ('I had the LORD always before me; because he is at my right hand, I shall not be shaken') as a resurrection psalm is Peter's most daring exegetical move. The entire Ps 16 meditation on the confident trust of the righteous one who expects God to 'not abandon my soul to Hades nor let your Holy One see corruption' is applied to Jesus's resurrection. The Christological argument: David wrote this psalm but died and his tomb remains (Acts 2:29); the psalm therefore looks forward to David's greater son who would in fact be raised without seeing corruption.
26: 'Therefore my heart was glad, and my tongue rejoiced; my flesh also will dwell in hope' — the resurrection hope that Ps 16's speaker expresses is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection. The Psalmist's confident hope ('my flesh will dwell in hope') becomes the description of Christ's resurrection body — his flesh was indeed raised, not 'in hope' only but in fact. The typological argument connects the pattern of righteous suffering-and-vindication across the Psalter to its unique historical fulfillment in Jesus.
27: 'For you will not abandon my soul to Hades, or let your Holy One see corruption' — 'Holy One' (hosios) is a divine designation that Peter applies to Jesus (cf. Acts 3:14 — 'the Holy and Righteous One'). The specific promise — no corruption — is the basis for the resurrection argument: the tomb was empty because God's promise to his Holy One was kept. The biological fact (no decay) is simultaneously a Christological declaration: Jesus is the Holy One who uniquely warranted and received God's resurrection promise.
28: 'You have made known to me the paths of life; you will make me full of gladness with your presence' — the Psalmist's hope of 'paths of life' is Christ's resurrection path out of death. The gladness 'with your presence' corresponds to the ascended Christ's exaltation at the Father's right hand (Acts 2:33). The Christological trajectory of Ps 16 runs: faithful trust → death → resurrection → exaltation → life in God's presence. The psalm narrates the entire Christ-event from the perspective of the righteous sufferer who trusts in God's vindication.
29: 'Brothers, I may say to you with confidence about the patriarch David that he both died and was buried, and his tomb is with us to this day' — Peter's argument hinges on the empirical fact that David's tomb was known and occupied. The Christological exegesis requires this: if David died and his body decayed, then Ps 16's promise of 'no corruption' cannot refer to David himself. The unfulfilled promise in David's tomb drives the argument toward the one in whom the promise was kept — Jesus, whose tomb was empty.
30: 'Being therefore a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him that he would set one of his descendants on his throne' — the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 7:12-16; Psalms 89:3-4; Psalms 132:11) provides the second prophetic line in Peter's argument. David knew, as a prophet, that the messianic promise involved a descendant who would reign forever. The Christological connection: the resurrection-exaltation of Jesus (Acts 2:33-34) is simultaneously the fulfillment of the resurrection promise (Ps 16) and the enthronement promise (Psalms 110:1) that define the Davidic covenant's ultimate scope.
31: 'He foresaw and spoke about the resurrection of the Christ, that he was not abandoned to Hades, and his flesh did not see corruption' — Peter's conclusion: David, the psalmist-prophet, was speaking about Christ's resurrection when he wrote Ps 16. The distinction between David's own death (Acts 2:29) and his prophetic foresight (Acts 2:30-31) establishes the typological hermeneutic of Acts' entire approach to the OT: the Psalms of the righteous sufferer are prophetic descriptions of Christ that exceed their Davidic context.
32: 'This Jesus God raised up, and of that we are all witnesses' — the proclamation formula is simple and historical. The Christological claim rests on testimony: 'we are all witnesses'. The resurrection is not a theological abstraction but an attested fact. Paul will use the same testimonial structure in 1 Corinthians 15:3-8 — 'he appeared to Cephas, then to the Twelve… then to more than five hundred brothers at one time'. The historicity of the resurrection is the foundation on which the entire Christological edifice stands.
33: 'Being therefore exalted at the right hand of God (tē dexia tou theou hypsōtheis), and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing and hearing.' The Christological movement: crucifixion → resurrection → exaltation → Spirit-pouring. The Pentecost event that the crowd is witnessing is itself evidence of the exaltation of Christ. The visible, audible Spirit-outpouring is the exalted Lord's signature in real time — the crowd cannot argue with what they are seeing and hearing.
34: 'For David did not ascend into the heavens, but he himself says, 'The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand'' — Psalms 110:1 applied to Christ's exaltation. As with Ps 16, Peter's argument is that David did not ascend to heaven (his tomb proves it), therefore Psalms 110:1's 'my Lord' seated at YHWH's right hand is not David but David's greater son. The Christological use of Ps 110 is the most widespread OT citation in the NT (cited in Matthew 22:44; Mark 12:36; Luke 20:42-43; 1 Corinthians 15:25; Ephesians 1:20; Hebrews 1:13, etc.) — the foundational proof-text for Christ's exaltation and lordship.
35: 'Until I make your enemies your footstool' — the continuing session at the Father's right hand, the ongoing subjugation of all opposition, will culminate in the parousia. The Christological eschatology: Christ currently reigns (Psalms 110:1 — 'sit') while the subjugation is completed (the 'until' clause), and will return when the Father's purpose is complete. The church exists in the 'until' — between the enthronement and the footstool's completion.
37: 'They were cut to the heart (katenygesan tēn kardian) and said, 'Brothers, what shall we do?'' — the Spirit-driven conviction of the crowd is the Christological sermon's intended effect. The question mirrors the Baptist's hearers (Luke 3:10, 12, 14) and foreshadows the Philippian jailer (Acts 16:30). The Christological response is consistent: the crisis of recognition ('you crucified the Lord') requires a response that names Jesus as both the source of the problem and the solution to it.
38: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit' — the Christological formula for entry into the new community. Repentance reorients the person toward the one they crucified; baptism in Jesus's name publicly identifies them with the crucified-and-risen Lord; forgiveness is specifically through Christ's name; the Spirit is the Christological gift of the new age. The entire salvation package is Christocentrically structured.
39: 'The promise is for you and for your children and for all who are far off, everyone whom the Lord our God calls to himself' — the scope of salvation extends temporally (children) and spatially ('far off' — a phrase from Isaiah 57:19 that Paul applies to Gentiles in Ephesians 2:13, 17). The Christological promise made at Pentecost is universal in scope from the first day: the salvation accomplished by Christ is offered without qualification across every human boundary. The limitation is only 'everyone whom the Lord our God calls' — election, not ethnicity.
40: 'And with many other words he bore witness and continued to exhort them, saying, 'Save yourselves from this crooked generation'' — the call to separate from the generation that rejected Christ echoes the OT wilderness Israel's opportunity to follow or reject God (Num 14). The Christological urgency: the judgment on Jerusalem (coming in 70 CE) hangs over those who reject the Pentecostal proclamation. Salvation is not only personal (forgiveness) but historical (escape from the coming judgment on the Christ-rejecting generation).
41: 'Those who received his word were baptized, and there were added that day about three thousand souls' — the three thousand added at Pentecost contrasts dramatically with the three thousand who fell in the golden calf incident (Exodus 32:28 — the day the law was given at Sinai). The Christological typology: where the old covenant's first day brought death, the new covenant's first day (Pentecost, the traditional date of Torah-giving) brings life. The Spirit enlivens where the letter kills (2 Corinthians 3:6).
42: 'They devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers' — the four marks of the Pentecost community are Christologically structured: (1) apostolic teaching is teaching about Christ; (2) koinōnia is fellowship in Christ's body; (3) breaking of bread is the eucharist — re-presentation of Christ's death (Luke 22:19 — 'do this in remembrance of me'); (4) prayers are in Christ's name. Every dimension of the early community's life is centered on the crucified-and-risen Lord.
43: 'Awe came upon every soul, and many wonders and signs were being done through the apostles' — the continuing miraculous works of the apostles are explicitly Christ's own works continued through them. Jesus had promised 'whoever believes in me will also do the works that I do; and greater works than these will he do' (John 14:12). The Christological mediation: every apostolic sign is performed 'through' them (implying Christ as the ultimate agent) and in Christ's name (Acts 3:6, 16; Acts 4:10).
44: 'All who believed were together and had all things in common' (eichon apanta koina) — the communal sharing of the Jerusalem church is a spontaneous expression of new-covenant reality: if Christ has abolished the boundary between Jew and Gentile (the ultimate social barrier), the lesser barriers of property and wealth are similarly negotiable. The Christological basis for economic sharing is eschatological: those who believe the kingdom has arrived live as if the ultimate redistribution has already occurred.
45: 'They were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need' — voluntary distribution according to need (not equality of outcome but responsiveness to deprivation) is the practical expression of the Christological community ethic. The pattern anticipates the Macedonian churches' generosity for the Jerusalem poor that Paul celebrates in 2 Cor 8-9 — giving as a response to Christ's own self-impoverishment ('though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor', 2 Corinthians 8:9).
46: 'Day by day, attending the temple together and breaking bread in their homes, they received their food with glad and generous hearts' — the dual practice of temple worship and house eucharist reflects the community's dual identity: still Israel, worshipping in the temple; also the new creation community gathered around Christ's table. The Christological ambiguity is temporary; by Acts 7-8 the temple relationship will be strained; by 70 CE the temple itself will be destroyed. The breaking of bread in homes is the durable, portable, post-temple form of Christ's communal presence.
47: 'Praising God and having favor with all the people. And the Lord added to their number day by day those who were being saved' — the growth of the community is specifically the Lord's action: ho kyrios prosetithei (the Lord was adding). The Christological sovereignty over the church's growth is consistent throughout Acts: Paul plants, Apollos waters, but God gives the growth (1 Corinthians 3:6). The community's attractive quality — 'favor with all the people' — is itself a Christological sign: those who live in Christ's kingdom are an appealing alternative to the world's anxious competition.
